english: A Finanzamt letter sits on the table. One single sentence. Forty-seven words long. Verumius understands nothing. Welcome to the world of German bureaucracy.
Learn 20 core words of official language
Recognize the difference between Amt, Behörde, and Verwaltung
Understand why Amtsdeutsch is its own language

Verumius's apartment. Evening. A letter on the kitchen table. The envelope says 'Finanzamt Berlin-Mitte.' The paper is official, the font is small, and the sentence is... one. One single sentence. Forty-seven words long.
That... is a sentence? A single sentence?

The sentence contains three subordinate clauses, two participial constructions, a genitive chain, and exactly zero comforting words. Somewhere in the middle it says 'Finanzamt.' Somewhere near the end: 'Frist.' Those are the only words Verumius recognizes.
I survived a panel discussion in German. I spoke in front of an audience. But this sentence? This sentence I do not understand.
the office (government), the authority
Neuter noun. Plural: die Aemter.\n'Finanzamt' (tax office), 'Bürgeramt' (citizens' office).

A: Where do I have to hand in the application?
B: At the office. Which office depends.
A: Tax office or citizens' office?
B: Tax office. It is about taxes.
From Old High German 'ambaht' (service). Germany has an Amt for everything. The -amt suffix is your clue: official government business. Finanzamt, Bürgeramt, Ordnungsamt, Bauamt.
the authority, the government agency
Feminine noun. Plural: die Behörden.\n'Die zuständige Behörde' (the responsible authority).

A: Which authority is responsible?
B: The foreigners' authority. They process residence permits.
A: And if I have problems?
B: Then you write a complaint to the authority.
From 'behören' (to belong to). More formal than Amt. A Behörde is the institution. An Amt is the office where you go. 'Die zuständige Behörde' is the standard phrase in official letters.
the official notice, the decision
Masculine noun. Plural: die Bescheide.\n'Steuerbescheid' (tax assessment notice).

A: Did you get the notice from the tax office?
B: Yes, but I do not understand it.
A: A Bescheid is an official decision.
B: Then I need help understanding it.
From 'bescheiden' (to inform/decide). A Bescheid is legally binding. When a Behörde sends you a Bescheid, it is an official act with legal force. Not just information, but a decision.
the form
Neuter noun. Plural: die Formulare.\n'Das Formular ausfüllen' (to fill out the form).

A: Where do I get the form?
B: Online or directly at the office.
A: Do I have to fill out everything?
B: Yes, every field. Otherwise it will be rejected.
From Latin 'formularium.' Germans fill out Formulare for everything: tax, residence, dog ownership, marriage. 'Das Formular ausfüllen' is the national pastime of German bureaucracy.
the application, the request
Masculine noun. Plural: die Anträge.\n'Einen Antrag stellen' (to file an application).

A: Have you filed the application yet?
B: No, I have to fill out the form first.
A: Hurry up, the deadline is approaching.
B: I know. Without an application, nothing happens.
'An' (at/on) + 'Trag' (carrying). Everything in German bureaucracy begins with an Antrag. No Antrag, no action. The gateway document to every official process.
the deadline, the time limit
Feminine noun. Plural: die Fristen.\n'Die Frist einhalten' (to meet the deadline).

A: When does the deadline expire?
B: On June 15th. Not a day later.
A: What happens if I miss the deadline?
B: Then it costs extra. Or you lose your right.
From Old High German 'frist' (time period). Missing a Frist in German bureaucracy can cost money or rights. 'Fristgerecht' means on time. Take every Frist seriously.
the approval, the permit
Feminine noun. Plural: die Genehmigungen.\n'Baugenehmigung' (building permit).

A: Do I have a permit for the renovation?
B: No, you first need the permit from the building office.
A: How long does that take?
B: Weeks. Sometimes months.
From 'genehmigen' (to approve). Without Genehmigung, you cannot build, stay, or park. Germany runs on Genehmigungen. 'Aufenthaltsgenehmigung' (residence permit) is the big one for foreigners.
the administration
Feminine noun. 'Die öffentliche Verwaltung' (public administration).\nFrom 'verwalten' (to manage).

A: Who is responsible for the administration?
B: The city administration takes care of it.
A: The administration is complicated.
B: Yes, but it works. Mostly.
From 'verwalten' (to manage/administer). The machinery that makes government work. Over 5 million people work in German Verwaltung. It is the engine behind every Amt.
the regulation, the rule
Feminine noun. Plural: die Vorschriften.\n'Sicherheitsvorschriften' (safety regulations).

A: May I park here?
B: No, there is a regulation against it.
A: Which regulation?
B: The road traffic regulations. Paragraph 12.
'Vor' (before/pre) + 'Schrift' (writing). Literally: what is written before (prescribed). Germany famously has Vorschriften for everything, from building codes to quiet hours.
the decree, the ordinance
Feminine noun. Plural: die Verordnungen.\n'Rechtsverordnung' (legal ordinance).

A: What is a Verordnung?
B: A rule from the government. Binding, but not a law.
A: How does it differ from a Vorschrift?
B: A Verordnung is stronger. It comes from above.
From 'verordnen' (to decree). More binding than a Vorschrift, less than a Gesetz (law). The middle layer of German legal hierarchy. Verordnungen can be issued by the executive branch.
the duty, the obligation
Feminine noun. Plural: die Pflichten.\n'Rechte und Pflichten' (rights and duties).

A: Is that a duty or voluntary?
B: A duty. You must do it.
A: And if I do not do it?
B: Then there are consequences. Duty is duty.
From Old High German 'pfliht' (care/duty). Deeply German: Kant's 'Pflicht' is the foundation of his moral philosophy. 'Pflichtversicherung' (compulsory insurance). Duty before desire.
the right, the law
Neuter noun. Plural: die Rechte.\n'Rechte und Pflichten' (rights and duties).

A: Do I have the right to object?
B: Yes, every citizen has the right.
A: Where does it say that?
B: In the law. Rights and duties go together.

From Old High German 'reht' (straight/right). Always paired with Pflicht in German legal thinking. You have Rechte, but they come with Pflichten. 'Rechtsstaat' (constitutional state) is a core German value.
the case worker, the clerk (m)
Masculine noun. 'Sache' (matter) + 'Bearbeiter' (processor).\n'Ihr zuständiger Sachbearbeiter' (your assigned case worker).

A: Who is processing my application?
B: Your assigned case worker, Mr Mueller.
A: Can I call him directly?
B: Only during office hours.
'Sache' (matter) + 'Bearbeiter' (processor). The person at the Behörde who handles YOUR file. The human behind the bureaucracy. Every case has one assigned Sachbearbeiter.
the case worker, the clerk (f)
Feminine noun. Sachbearbeiter + '-in.'\nStandard in all government correspondence.

A: Who is my case worker?
B: Mrs Schmidt is your case worker.
A: And when does she have office hours?
B: Tuesdays and Thursdays, 9 to 12.
Sachbearbeiter + '-in.' Official letters often use both: 'Ihr Sachbearbeiter / Ihre Sachbearbeiterin.' Gender-inclusive language is standard in all German government correspondence.
the citizen (m)
Masculine noun. Plural: die Bürger.\n'Bürgerrechte' (civil rights).

A: As a citizen you have rights.
B: What rights does a citizen have?
A: Right to vote, freedom of assembly, freedom of speech.
B: And duties?
From 'Burg' (castle/town). Originally: someone who lives in a Burg (fortified town). 'Bürgerrechte' (civil rights). The fundamental identity in German democracy.
the citizen (f)
Feminine noun. Bürger + '-in.'\n'Bürgerinnen und Bürger' (citizens, f/m).

A: Every female citizen has the right to education.
B: And every male citizen too.
A: Female and male citizens are equal before the law.
B: That is what the constitution says.
Bürger + '-in.' 'Bürgerinnen und Bürger' is the standard opening in political speeches. Always both forms, always in that order. Gender-inclusive language in action.
official
Adjective. 'Amt' + '-lich.'\n'Amtliche Dokumente' (official documents).

A: Is this document official?
B: Yes, it bears the official stamp.
A: Then it is legally binding?
B: Yes. Officially certified.
'Amt' + '-lich.' The adjective that turns any piece of paper into a legally binding document. 'Amtlich beglaubigt' (officially certified) is the gold standard of German paperwork.
bureaucratic
Adjective. From French 'bureaucratique.'\n'Der buerokratische Aufwand' (the bureaucratic burden).

A: Why is this so bureaucratic?
B: Welcome to Germany.
A: Is there no simpler way?
B: No. The bureaucratic way is the only way.
From French 'bureaucratique.' Germans both love and hate their Bürokratie. It works, but it is heavy. 'Bürokratieabbau' (reducing bureaucracy) is every government's promise.
legal, statutory
Adjective. 'Gesetz' (law) + '-lich.'\n'Gesetzlich vorgeschrieben' (required by law).

A: Is health insurance required by law?
B: Yes, in Germany it is mandatory.
A: For everyone?
B: For everyone. Regulated by law.
'Gesetz' (law) + '-lich.' When something is gesetzlich, there is no negotiating. 'Die gesetzliche Krankenversicherung' (statutory health insurance) covers about 90% of Germans.
in writing, written
Adjective. 'Schrift' (writing) + '-lich.'\n'Bitte bestätigen Sie schriftlich' (please confirm in writing).

A: Can I explain this orally?
B: No, it must be in writing.
A: Why always in writing?
B: In bureaucracy, only what is written counts.
'Schrift' (writing) + '-lich.' In German bureaucracy, if it is not schriftlich, it did not happen. Oral agreements mean nothing to a Behörde. Always get it in writing.
When an authority sends you a Bescheid, it is a legally binding decision.
The document you submit to an authority to request something. The gateway to bureaucracy.
Verumius must submit the application by June 15th.
He survived a public debate. But Amtsdeutsch has its own grammar: one sentence that never ends.
Learn the names of German offices and authorities. Understand deadlines, permits, and appeal procedures. Build your bureaucratic survival kit.
Learn 20 new words for offices, deadlines, and procedures
Understand the system of objection and appeal
Navigate the German Amt system confidently
the citizens' office
Neuter noun. 'Bürger' (citizen) + 'Amt' (office).\nFirst stop after arriving in Germany: Anmeldung.

A: I just moved to Berlin.
B: Then you have to go to the citizens' office. Registration.
A: What do I need?
B: ID and rental contract. Do not forget the appointment.
Every person in Germany must register their address within 14 days of moving. No registration, no bank account, no phone contract. The Bürgeramt is where German life officially begins.
the tax office
Neuter noun. 'Finanz' (finance) + 'Amt' (office).\nEvery city has one. They send Bescheide and collect taxes.

A: I have mail from the tax office.
B: Tax assessment notice?
A: I think so. 47 words, one sentence.
B: Typical tax office.
Every German city has a Finanzamt. They send Bescheide, collect taxes, and terrify residents with dense legal prose. 'Ich muss zum Finanzamt' is a sentence nobody says with joy.
the building office
Neuter noun. 'Bau' (building) + 'Amt' (office).\n'Baugenehmigung' (building permit).

A: I want to build a conservatory.
B: Then you need a permit from the building office.
A: Even for a small conservatory?
B: In Germany you need a building permit for almost everything.
'Bau' (building/construction) + 'Amt.' Germany regulates construction at every level. The Bauamt decides what you can and cannot build. Even a garden shed may need a Baugenehmigung.
the public order office
Neuter noun. 'Ordnung' (order) + 'Amt' (office).\nHandles noise complaints, illegal parking, unregistered dogs.

A: My neighbor makes noise every evening.
B: Call the public order office.
A: Can they do something?
B: Yes, they can impose a fine.
'Ordnung' (order) + 'Amt.' The Ordnungsamt handles everything that disturbs public order: noise, parking violations, unregistered dogs. They can issue Bussgelder (fines).
the city hall
Neuter noun. 'Rat' (council) + 'Haus' (house).\nWhere the Bürgermeister works.

A: Where is the city administration?
B: In city hall. Right at the market square.
A: Is city hall always this big?
B: In Germany it is often the most beautiful building in town.
'Rat' (council) + 'Haus' (house). Every German city, no matter how small, has a Rathaus. Often the most beautiful building in town. The Bürgermeister (mayor) works here.
the registration certificate
Feminine noun. 'Meldung' (registration) + 'Bescheinigung' (certificate).\nProof of address registration.

A: I need a registration certificate.
B: You get that at the citizens' office.
A: What do I need it for?
B: For everything: bank account, phone contract, work permit.

'Meldung' (registration) + 'Bescheinigung' (certificate). Proof that you registered your address. You need it for bank accounts, phone contracts, and work permits. The first German document every newcomer gets.
the residence permit
Feminine noun. 'Aufenthalt' (stay) + 'Erlaubnis' (permission).\nFor non-EU citizens.

A: My residence permit is expiring soon.
B: Then you must renew it. Quickly.
A: At which authority?
B: At the foreigners' authority. Make an appointment.
'Aufenthalt' (stay/residence) + 'Erlaubnis' (permission). For non-EU citizens, this document is life in Germany. Without it, you cannot work, rent, or stay. The holy grail of German immigration.
the foreigners' registration office
Feminine noun. 'Ausländer' (foreigner) + 'Behörde' (authority).\nNow often called 'Aufenthaltsbehörde.'

A: Where do I apply for the residence permit?
B: At the foreigners' registration office.
A: Do I need an appointment?
B: Absolutely. Nothing works without an appointment.

'Ausländer' (foreigner) + 'Behörde' (authority). Now often called 'Aufenthaltsbehörde' (residence authority). Where non-EU citizens apply for and renew their residence permits. Long waits are legendary.
the tax return
Feminine noun. 'Steuer' (tax) + 'Erklarung' (declaration).\n'Die Steuererklarung abgeben' (to file a tax return).

A: Have you filed your tax return yet?
B: No, the deadline is not until July.
A: Do you do it yourself or with a tax advisor?
B: With a tax advisor. It is too complicated for me.
'Steuer' (tax) + 'Erklarung' (declaration). Germans who are required to file have until July 31 (or extended with a Steuerberater). Many Germans love their Steuererklarung because refunds are common.
the fine
Neuter noun. 'Busse' (penance) + 'Geld' (money).\n'Ein Bussgeld zahlen' (to pay a fine).

A: I got a fine.
B: For what?
A: Parked wrong. 55 euros.
B: That is still cheap. Next time it will be more expensive.
'Busse' (penance/fine) + 'Geld' (money). For traffic violations, late registrations, missed deadlines. The Ordnungsamt and Finanzamt both issue Bussgelder. Germany's way of enforcing Ordnung.
the regulatory offense
Feminine noun. 'Ordnung' (order) + 'Widrigkeit' (contravention).\nLess than criminal, but you pay.

A: Is illegal parking a criminal offense?
B: No, a regulatory offense.
A: What is the difference?
B: A regulatory offense is not criminal, but you pay.

'Ordnung' (order) + 'Widrigkeit' (contravention). A uniquely German legal category between 'no problem' and 'criminal.' Parking wrong, skipping registration, noise after 10pm. Not criminal, but expensive.
the reminder, the dunning notice
Feminine noun. From 'mahnen' (to remind/urge).\n'Erste, zweite, dritte Mahnung.'

A: I got a dunning notice.
B: The first or the second?
A: The second.
B: Then pay quickly. After the third comes the lawyer.
From 'mahnen' (to remind/urge). Three chances: erste Mahnung, zweite Mahnung, letzte Mahnung. After the third, legal action. The escalation system of German payment culture.
the objection, the appeal
Masculine noun. 'Ein' (in) + 'Spruch' (statement).\n'Einspruch erheben' (to raise an objection).

A: I do not agree with the tax assessment.
B: Then you can raise an objection.
A: How do I do that?
B: In writing. Within the deadline.
'Ein' (in) + 'Spruch' (statement). If you disagree with a Bescheid, you can Einspruch erheben. But respect the Frist. Your legal right to say: I do not accept this decision.
the formal objection, the contradiction
Masculine noun. 'Wider' (against) + 'Spruch' (statement).\n'Widerspruch einlegen' (to file an objection).

A: What is the difference between Einspruch and Widerspruch?
B: Widerspruch is broader. Administrative law.
A: And how do I file an objection?
B: In writing to the authority. Respect the deadline.
'Wider' (against) + 'Spruch' (statement). Slightly broader than Einspruch. Used in administrative law. Your right to say officially: I disagree with this decision.
the complaint
Feminine noun. From 'beschweren' (to complain).\n'Eine Beschwerde einreichen' (to file a complaint).

A: The case worker was unfriendly.
B: Then file a complaint.
A: With whom?
B: With the supervisor or the complaints office.
From 'beschweren' (to burden/complain). Less formal than Widerspruch. You can Beschwerde einreichen about service quality, but Widerspruch einlegen against an official decision.
the approval, the grant
Feminine noun. From 'bewilligen' (to grant).\n'Die Bewilligung erteilen' (to grant approval).

A: Has the authority granted the approval?
B: Yes, the approval is here.
A: Finally. How long did that take?
B: Three months. But now it is approved.
From 'bewilligen' (to grant/approve). Synonym for Genehmigung but often used for financial approvals. Budget-Bewilligung is the green light for spending public money.
limited in time, fixed-term
Adjective. From 'befristen' (to set a deadline).\n'Ein befristeter Vertrag' (a fixed-term contract).

A: Is your contract fixed-term?
B: Yes, limited to two years.
A: And after that?
B: Then I hope for a permanent contract.
From 'befristen' (to set a deadline). 'Befristete Aufenthaltserlaubnis' (temporary residence permit). The clock is always ticking. In German work life, befristet is the starting point.
unlimited, permanent
Adjective. 'Un-' + 'befristet.'\n'Ein unbefristeter Arbeitsvertrag' (a permanent contract).

A: I got a permanent contract!
B: Congratulations! That is the dream.
A: Now I can buy an apartment.
B: Exactly. The bank loves permanent contracts.
'Un-' + 'befristet.' The holy grail of German work life. Security, stability, bank loans. Unbefristet means: no end date. The word every employee wants on their Arbeitsvertrag.
on time, within the deadline
Adjective. 'Frist' (deadline) + 'gerecht' (proper).\n'Fristgerecht einreichen' (to submit on time).

A: Did you submit the application on time?
B: Yes, three days before the deadline.
A: Good. Later would have been too late.
B: I know. On time is essential for survival.
'Frist' (deadline) + 'gerecht' (just/proper). In German bureaucracy, fristgerecht is not a suggestion. It is survival. Miss the Frist, lose your rights.
Adjective. From 'gelten' (to be valid).\n'Der Ausweis ist noch gueltig' (the ID is still valid).

A: Is your passport still valid?
B: Yes, still valid for two years.
A: Good. No authority accepts expired documents.
B: Everything must be valid. Always.
From 'gelten' (to be valid). Everything in Germany has a Gueltigkeit (validity period). Permits, IDs, tickets, contracts. 'Bis wann ist das gueltig?' is a question you will ask often.

Germany has a three-tier administration: Bund (federal), Land (state), Kommune (local). Each level has its own offices.

Bürgeramt = local (address, registration)
Finanzamt = state level (taxes)
Ausländerbehörde = can be either

Rule of thumb: address = local. Money = state. Foreigners = check the city.

Always bring: Personalausweis or passport, Meldebescheinigung, and patience.

German federalism distributes power across three levels. This is historical: after 1945, no single body should ever again hold all the power. The result is a system that works slowly, but democratically. Each office has clear areas of responsibility. Overlaps are intentional, not accidental.
The local office that handles address registration. Every move to a new address must be reported there within 14 days.
The formal document you lodge when you disagree with an official decision. Think: you push back against the ruling.
If you do not agree with the notice, you can file an objection.
'Frist' means deadline. 'Befristet' means: there is an end date. The clock is ticking.
45 minutes on hold. Beethoven on endless loop. Learn the language of bureaucratic automation and master the Zustandspassiv.

Decode the impersonal constructions of Amtsdeutsch
Verumius's apartment. The phone is on speaker. Beethoven's 'Für Elise' plays. Again. And again. The call timer shows 00:45:12. Verumius has moved from sitting to standing to stretching to yoga.
Please be patient for a moment. Your call is important to us. You will be connected to the next available case worker.
My call is important to you. For 45 minutes.
Please note: The file reference number must be stated in all correspondence. The deadline expired on June 15th. The notice has been delivered to you.
the processing fee
Feminine noun. 'Bearbeitung' (processing) + 'Gebühr' (fee).\n'Bearbeitungsgebühr: 25 Euro.'

A: Why do I have to pay a fee?
B: That is the processing fee.
A: For what exactly?
B: For processing your application. 25 euros.
'Bearbeitung' (processing) + 'Gebühr' (fee). You pay the Behörde for the privilege of them processing your Antrag. The price of bureaucracy, calculated to the cent.
the file reference number
Neuter noun. 'Akte' (file) + 'Zeichen' (sign/mark).\n'Bitte geben Sie Ihr Aktenzeichen an.'

A: What is your file reference number?
B: One moment, I am looking for it. Here: AZ-2026-4471.
A: Please state it in all correspondence.
B: Without the reference number they cannot find my case?
'Akte' (file) + 'Zeichen' (sign/mark). Every official letter has one. Lose it, and the Behörde cannot find your case. Your bureaucratic identity in one code.
the certificate, the attestation
Feminine noun. From 'bescheinigen' (to certify).\n'Arbeitgeberbescheinigung' (employer's certificate).

A: I need a certificate from my employer.
B: For what?
A: For the residence permit.
B: Then please request an employer's certificate.
From 'bescheinigen' (to certify). Official paper that confirms something. More specific than Formular, less binding than Bescheid. Germany runs on Bescheinigungen.
the confirmation of receipt
Feminine noun. 'Eingang' (receipt) + 'Bestätigung' (confirmation).\nProof the Behörde received your documents.

A: Did you receive my application?
B: Yes, you will receive a confirmation of receipt by mail.
A: How long does that take?
B: About two weeks. Then you wait.
'Eingang' (receipt/entry) + 'Bestätigung' (confirmation). The bureaucracy telling you: we received your paper. Now wait. An Eingangsbestätigung is not a decision, just an acknowledgment.
the issuance, the granting
Feminine noun. From 'erteilen' (to grant/issue).\n'Die Erteilung der Genehmigung' (the issuance of the permit).

A: When will the permit be issued?
B: After examination of all documents.
A: And how long does the examination take?
B: Four to six weeks.
From 'erteilen' (to grant/issue). The formal noun for when the Behörde says yes. Erteilung = you got it. The moment your Antrag becomes a Genehmigung.
the delivery (of official documents)
Feminine noun. From 'zustellen' (to deliver).\nLegal term: Fristen start from Zustellung.

A: The notice was delivered to you on May 1st.
B: The delivery was on May 1st?
A: Yes, deadlines start from delivery.
B: Then I have 14 more days for the objection.
From 'zustellen' (to deliver). Legal term: the moment the document officially reaches you. Fristen start from Zustellung. The postman becomes a legal actor.
the administrative act
Masculine noun. 'Verwaltung' + 'Akt' (act).\nLegal term for any official decision by a Behörde.

A: What exactly is an administrative act?
B: Any official decision by an authority.
A: For example?
B: A tax assessment. A building permit. A rejection.
'Verwaltung' + 'Akt' (act). The building block of German administrative law. Every Verwaltungsakt must be justified. If it is not, you can challenge it with a Widerspruch.
the facts of the matter
Masculine noun. 'Sache' (matter) + 'Verhalt' (state of affairs).\n'Den Sachverhalt prüfen' (to examine the facts).

A: Please describe the facts of the matter.
B: I received the letter on May 5th.
A: And what exactly is the problem?
B: The amount does not match my calculation.
'Sache' (matter) + 'Verhalt' (state of affairs). Legal vocabulary: before deciding, establish the Sachverhalt. The facts, the circumstances, the objective truth of what happened.
the legal situation
Feminine noun. 'Recht' (law) + 'Lage' (situation).\n'Die aktuelle Rechtslage' (the current legal situation).

A: What is the legal situation in my case?
B: The legal situation is clear: you must pay.
A: Is there no other way?
B: The legal situation leaves no room for interpretation.
'Recht' (law) + 'Lage' (situation). What the law says RIGHT NOW about your case. Can change if laws change. 'Die aktuelle Rechtslage' is the phrase lawyers use to anchor their advice.
the procedure, the process
Neuter noun. From 'verfahren' (to proceed).\n'Ein Verwaltungsverfahren' (an administrative procedure).

A: How does the procedure work?
B: First application, then examination, then notice.
A: How long does the whole procedure take?
B: Between four weeks and six months.
From 'verfahren' (to proceed). The formal path from Antrag to Bescheid. Also: 'Gerichtsverfahren' (court proceedings). The structured journey through the bureaucratic machine.
the amount (of money)
Masculine noun. From 'betragen' (to amount to).\n'Der zu zahlende Betrag' (the amount to be paid).

A: How much is the amount?
B: The amount is 300 euros.
A: 300 euros? That is a lot.
B: The amount is unfortunately not negotiable.
From 'betragen' (to amount to). Official letters always specify the Betrag: how much you owe, to the cent. 'Der zu zahlende Betrag' is the phrase that makes your wallet nervous.
the duration, the term
Feminine noun. 'Lauf' (run) + 'Zeit' (time).\n'Die Laufzeit des Vertrags' (the duration of the contract).

A: How long is the duration of the contract?
B: Duration: 12 months.
A: Can I cancel earlier?
B: Only with a notice period of three months.
'Lauf' (run) + 'Zeit' (time). How long something runs. Contracts, loans, permits all have a Laufzeit. The clock starts the moment you sign.
the section of law
Masculine noun. From Greek 'paragraphos.'\n'Gemaess Paragraph 3' (in accordance with paragraph 3).

A: In which paragraph does it say that?
B: Paragraph 3 of the income tax law.
A: Can I look that up?
B: Yes, all laws are available online.
From Greek 'paragraphos' (written beside). Paragraphen are numbered, cited, and argued over. 'Gemaess Paragraph 5' is the citation formula that gives legal authority to any bureaucratic statement.
the official channels
Masculine noun. 'Dienst' (service) + 'Weg' (way/path).\n'Auf dem Dienstweg' (through official channels).

A: Can I speak directly with the boss?
B: No, that has to go through official channels.
A: What does that mean?
B: First to the case worker, then to the supervisor.
'Dienst' (service) + 'Weg' (way/path). The proper bureaucratic path. Skipping the Dienstweg is frowned upon. Follow the road, step by step, level by level.
it concerns, this is about
Impersonal construction.\n'Es handelt sich um eine Nachzahlung.'

A: What is the letter about?
B: It concerns an additional tax payment.
A: So I have to pay money?
B: Yes, it concerns 300 euros.
The official way to introduce what a letter is about. Impersonal, distanced, institutional. 'Es handelt sich um' removes any personal tone and replaces it with bureaucratic neutrality.
it is to be assumed that
Impersonal construction.\nBureaucratic certainty phrased as assumption.

A: Is the notice correct?
B: It is to be assumed that the notice is correct.
A: What does that mean in simple language?
B: It means: Yes, it is correct. Prove otherwise.
Bureaucratic certainty phrased as polite assumption. Translation: this is true until you prove otherwise. 'Es ist davon auszugehen' is the Behörde saying: we are right.
it requires (+ genitive)
Impersonal + genitive construction.\n'Es bedarf einer Genehmigung' (a permit is required).

A: Do I need a permit?
B: Yes, a written permit is required.
A: Where do I get it?
B: At the responsible office. Of course.
The most formal way to say 'you need to.' Es bedarf + genitive is pure Amtsdeutsch. Beautiful, impersonal, non-negotiable. No subject, no person, just the requirement.
it applies, the following applies
Impersonal construction.\n'Es gilt die Frist zu beachten' (the deadline must be observed).

A: What do I need to be aware of?
B: The deadline must be observed.
A: Anything else?
B: Furthermore, all documents must be submitted.
'Es gilt' announces a rule. No subject needed. The rule exists independently of people. What follows is law. Impersonal, absolute, universal.
attention is drawn to the fact that
Konjunktiv I passive construction.\nThe politest possible threat in Amtsdeutsch.

A: What does it say at the end of the letter?
B: 'Attention is drawn to the fact that the deadline expires on the 15th.'
A: That sounds polite.
B: It is a polite threat. Pay by the 15th.
Konjunktiv I passive: 'sei' instead of 'ist.' Maximum formal distance. The politest possible way to say: your deadline is approaching. We are merely pointing it out.
in accordance with (+ dative)
Preposition. From 'Mass' (measure).\n'Gemaess Paragraph 5' (in accordance with paragraph 5).

A: In accordance with which law do I have to pay?
B: In accordance with paragraph 3 of the income tax law.
A: Can I look that up?
B: Yes, all laws are on the internet.
From 'Mass' (measure). The preposition of legal conformity. What the law says, you do. Can precede or follow its noun: 'gemaess dem Gesetz' or 'dem Gesetz gemaess.'

German has TWO passive forms that look similar but mean very different things.

- The Vorgangspassiv uses werden plus Partizip II and describes an ongoing action: 'Der Antrag wird bearbeitet' (is currently being processed).
- The Zustandspassiv uses sein plus Partizip II and describes the finished result: 'Der Antrag ist bearbeitet' (finished, done).

Official German loves the Zustandspassiv. 'Der Bescheid ist zugestellt,' 'Die Frist ist abgelaufen,' 'Das Verfahren ist eingeleitet.' Everything is presented as already done. The bureaucracy has already decided. The ball is in your court.

The authority presents results, not processes. 'Der Bescheid ist zugestellt' says: the delivery is a fact. You cannot claim you did not receive it. The Zustandspassiv creates a reality that you must challenge if you disagree.
Sein + Partizip II = the action is COMPLETE. The result exists as a state.
The form is filled out and done. The result is complete. Which auxiliary verb signals a finished state, not a process?
The form is already filled out.
'Es handelt sich um' is a fixed reflexive formula. It introduces the topic of a letter. What is the closest everyday equivalent in meaning?
KD reads the 47-word sentence in five seconds. 'Pay 300 euros.' That is all. Now you learn legal terminology and the subjectless passive.
Learn 22 legal terminology words
Master the subjectless passive (Es wird darauf hingewiesen...)
Distinguish binding and advisory language
The neighborhood cafe. Two coffees. Verumius slides the letter across the table. KD picks it up. His eyes scan it. Five seconds. He puts it down.
You have to pay 300 euros by June 15th. That is all.
That is ALL? Forty-seven words for 'Pay 300 euros'?
Welcome to the German administrative language. 'Attention is drawn to the fact that with reference to...' means: 'Pay.' Done.
the lawyer, the attorney (m)
Masculine noun. 'Recht' (law) + 'Anwalt' (advocate).\n'Einen Rechtsanwalt einschalten' (to engage a lawyer).

A: Do I need a lawyer?
B: For 300 euros? Probably not.
A: From what amount is it worthwhile?
B: That depends on the case. Initial consultation is often free.
'Recht' (law) + 'Anwalt' (advocate). Germany has over 160,000 Rechtsanwaelte. They have a monopoly on legal representation in court. No Rechtsanwalt, no court appearance.
the lawyer, the attorney (f)
Feminine noun. Rechtsanwalt + '-in.'\nOver 40% of new lawyers in Germany are women.

A: Do you know a good lawyer?
B: Yes, Dr. Weber. Specialist for administrative law.
A: Can I call her directly?
B: Yes, the law firm is available Monday to Friday.
Rechtsanwalt + '-in.' Over 40% of new lawyers in Germany are women. 'Frau Rechtsanwaeltin' is the formal address in court and correspondence.
the judge (m)
Masculine noun. From 'richten' (to judge/direct).\nGerman judges are career civil servants.

A: Who decides the case?
B: The judge.
A: Is the judge independent?
B: Yes, judges in Germany are independent and only bound by the law.

From 'richten' (to judge/direct). German judges are career civil servants, not elected. They train specifically for the judiciary after passing two state exams. A different path than in Anglo-Saxon systems.
the judge (f)
Feminine noun. Richter + '-in.'\nOver 40% of German judges are women.

A: Who is the judge in my case?
B: Judge Mueller at the administrative court.
A: Is she strict?
B: Fair. That is more important.
Richter + '-in.' Over 40% of German judges are women. The judiciary has been a leader in gender balance compared to other legal professions in Germany.
the plaintiff (m)
Masculine noun. From 'klagen' (to sue/complain).\n'Der Klaeger muss beweisen...' (The plaintiff must prove...).

A: Who is the plaintiff?
B: The plaintiff is the citizen who is suing against the notice.
A: And who is the defendant?
B: The authority that issued the notice.
From 'klagen' (to sue/complain). The person who brings a lawsuit. In German courts, the Klaeger has the burden of proof. Whoever klages, must prove.
the plaintiff (f)
Feminine noun. Klaeger + '-in.'\nStandard in court documents.

A: The plaintiff, Mrs Schmidt, is raising an objection.
B: Against what?
A: Against the tax assessment from the tax office.
B: The court will examine the case.
Klaeger + '-in.' Standard in court documents. German legal language always uses both forms in official judgments: 'der Klaeger / die Klaegerin.'
the judgment, the verdict
Neuter noun. From 'urteilen' (to judge).\n'Das Urteil ist gesprochen' (the verdict has been pronounced).

A: Has the court pronounced a verdict?
B: Yes, the verdict has been pronounced.
A: In whose favor?
B: In favor of the plaintiff.
From 'urteilen' (to judge). 'Im Namen des Volkes' (In the name of the people) opens every German court judgment. A Urteil is final at its Instanz unless you file Berufung.
the appeal (to a higher court)
Feminine noun. From 'berufen' (to call upon).\n'Berufung einlegen' (to file an appeal).

A: Can I appeal the verdict?
B: Yes, within one month after the verdict.
A: At which court?
B: At the next instance. The higher regional court.
From 'berufen' (to call upon). You call upon a higher court to review the decision. Also means 'calling/vocation.' In law: your right to challenge a judgment at a higher Instanz.
the legal instance, the court level
Feminine noun. From Latin 'instantia.'\n'Erste Instanz' (trial court), 'zweite Instanz' (appeals court).

A: At which instance is the case?
B: Still at the first instance. Local court.
A: And if I lose?
B: Then you can go to the second instance.
From Latin 'instantia.' Germany has up to three Instanzen. Each review can change the outcome. 'Erste Instanz' = trial court, 'zweite Instanz' = appeals court, 'dritte Instanz' = supreme court.
the legal remedy
Masculine noun. 'Recht' + 'Behelf' (remedy).\n'Rechtsbehelfsbelehrung' (instruction on legal remedies).

A: What legal remedies do I have?
B: Objection, lawsuit, or appeal.
A: Does it say that in the notice?
B: Yes, every notice must contain an instruction on legal remedies.

'Recht' + 'Behelf' (remedy). Every Bescheid must tell you which Rechtsbehelfe you have. Your right to fight back, legally. 'Rechtsbehelfsbelehrung' is the paragraph at the end of every official notice.
the liability
Feminine noun. 'Haft' (liability) + 'Pflicht' (duty).\n'Haftpflichtversicherung' (liability insurance).

A: Do you have liability insurance?
B: Of course. Everyone in Germany has one.
A: What does it cover?
B: Damage you accidentally cause to others.
'Haft' (liability) + 'Pflicht' (duty). Nearly every German has a Haftpflichtversicherung. It covers damage you accidentally cause to others. The most German insurance: covering your Pflicht.
Every Bescheid must tell you which options you have to fight back. This is your right to challenge an official decision.
Legal adjectives and insurance terms: binding, lawful, permissible, orderly. The language of German bureaucracy and legal compliance.
Learn legal adjectives
Master insurance and tax vocabulary
Describe compliance and regulations
the compulsory insurance
Feminine noun. 'Pflicht' + 'Versicherung' (insurance).\nHealth insurance, car insurance, liability insurance.

A: Which insurances are compulsory in Germany?
B: Health insurance and car insurance.
A: And liability?
B: Not legally compulsory, but everyone has one.

'Pflicht' + 'Versicherung.' Germany mandates several Versicherungen. Health insurance, car insurance. No choice. Pflicht is Pflicht. The German approach: collective safety through mandatory protection.
the property tax
Feminine noun. 'Grund' (ground/property) + 'Steuer' (tax).\nPaid by property owners to the local Gemeinde.

A: Do you have to pay property tax?
B: Yes, as a property owner I pay property tax.
A: How much?
B: Depends on the property value. The reform changed everything.
'Grund' (ground/property) + 'Steuer' (tax). Paid by property owners to the local Gemeinde. Recently reformed (Grundsteuerreform 2022). The tax on owning a piece of Germany.
the damages, the compensation
Masculine noun. 'Schaden' (damage) + 'Ersatz' (replacement).\n'Schadensersatz fordern' (to claim damages).

A: The neighbor damaged my car.
B: Then you can claim damages.
A: How?
B: In writing. Or through a lawyer.
'Schaden' (damage) + 'Ersatz' (replacement/compensation). You replace the damage with money. The legal principle of making whole: restoring what was broken through financial compensation.
legally binding
Adjective. 'Recht' + 'verbindlich' (binding).\n'Eine rechtsverbindliche Zusage' (a legally binding commitment).

A: Is this commitment legally binding?
B: Yes, it is legally binding.
A: What does that mean concretely?
B: If you break it, you can be sued.
'Recht' + 'verbindlich' (binding). When something is rechtsverbindlich, you can be sued if you break it. The word that transforms a promise into a legal obligation.
unlawful, illegal
Adjective. 'Recht' + 'widrig' (contrary to).\n'Ein rechtswidriger Verwaltungsakt' (an unlawful administrative act).

A: Can a notice be unlawful?
B: Yes, if it violates the law.
A: And what happens then?
B: Then it can be overturned. Via objection or lawsuit.
'Recht' + 'widrig' (contrary to). The adjective that invalidates official actions. If a Verwaltungsakt is rechtswidrig, it can be overturned. The citizen's most powerful word.
binding, obligatory; also: friendly
Adjective. From 'verbinden' (to bind).\nDouble meaning: legally binding AND socially warm.

A: Is the offer binding?
B: Yes, it is a binding offer.
A: Then I will sign.
B: Many thanks for your trust.
From 'verbinden' (to bind). Double meaning: legally binding AND socially warm. A 'verbindliches Angebot' is binding. A 'verbindlicher Ton' is friendly. Context decides.
permissible, admissible
Adjective. From 'zulassen' (to permit).\n'Ist die Klage zulaessig?' (Is the lawsuit admissible?).

A: Is my objection admissible?
B: If it was filed on time, yes.
A: And if not?
B: Then it will be rejected as inadmissible.
From 'zulassen' (to permit). The gatekeeper word in law: before the court considers your case, it checks if it is zulaessig. If not, the door closes before the argument even starts.
in accordance with regulations, proper
Adjective. 'Ordnung' + 'gemaess' (in accordance with).\n'Ordnungsgemaess eingereicht' (properly filed).

A: Was the application properly filed?
B: Yes, all documents are complete and on time.
A: Then everything is in order?
B: Proper means: everything according to regulations.
'Ordnung' + 'gemaess' (in accordance with). The bureaucratic seal of approval: you followed all the rules. 'Ordnungsgemaess eingereicht' means: we accept your paperwork.
comprehensible, understandable
Adjective. 'Nach' (after) + 'vollziehbar' (executable).\nYou can trace the logic step by step.

A: Is the decision comprehensible?
B: Yes, the reasoning is clear and comprehensible.
A: Not like the 47-word sentence?
B: No, this time it is truly comprehensible.
'Nach' (after) + 'vollziehbar' (executable/comprehensible). You can trace the logic step by step. The opposite of impenetrable Amtsdeutsch. Every good Bescheid should be nachvollziehbar.
legal, pertaining to law
Adjective. 'Recht' + '-lich.'\n'Rechtliche Schritte einleiten' (to take legal action).

A: Are there legal consequences?
B: Yes, if you miss the deadline.
A: What legal steps can the authority take?
B: Fine, dunning notice, enforcement.
'Recht' + '-lich.' The general adjective for anything law-related. 'Rechtlich gesehen...' (From a legal perspective...). The neutral word that frames everything in legal terms.
approved
Past participle of 'genehmigen.' Approved.\n'Der Antrag ist genehmigt.' (Zustandspassiv in action.)

A: Is the application approved?
B: Yes, the application is approved.
A: Finally!
B: Approved means: you may begin.
Past participle of 'genehmigen.' Zustandspassiv in action: 'Der Antrag ist genehmigt.' The word you want to see on any bureaucratic response. The green light of German officialdom.

Official German loves sentences without a real subject.

'Es wird darauf hingewiesen, dass...' (Who points it out? Nobody. Everyone. The institution.)
'Es wurde beschlossen, dass...' (Who decided? The system.)

The impersonal passive removes the human actor completely. The decision comes from the system, not from a person.

When 'es' stands in position 1, it is a placeholder (not a real subject). If something else occupies position 1, 'es' disappears: 'Hiermit wird darauf hingewiesen, dass...'

'Es' is only a placeholder for position 1. As soon as another word takes that position, 'es' drops out:

'Es wird gebeten...' but: 'Hiermit wird gebeten...'
'Es wurde beschlossen...' but: 'Gestern wurde beschlossen...'

This shows: 'es' is not a real subject, just a position holder. The sentence has no real subject. The action exists without an actor.
'Es wird gebeten' uses a passive construction without a named actor. No individual is identified. Which answer reflects that the request comes from an official body, not a specific human?
The delivery is complete, the result exists now. Choose the auxiliary that expresses a finished state, not an ongoing process.
The notice has already been delivered to you.
Impersonal passive (Es wird hingewiesen) plus Zustandspassiv (ist abgelaufen). Double passive power.
KD's translation: everything the letter really says, in one short sentence. Amount plus deadline.
KD decodes the patterns of Amtsdeutsch. Learn the genitive prepositions of the formal register, practice official appeals, and decode the reference formulas of bureaucratic language.
Master genitive prepositions in the formal register
Learn to formulate official appeals
Decode the reference patterns of Amtsdeutsch
Okay, Verumius. Official German has a system. If you know the patterns, it is like a foreign language with only 50 words.
Show me the patterns.
'Regarding your application' means: 'About your application.' 'With regard to the deadline' means: 'Because of the deadline.' 'Subject to approval' means: 'If the approval comes.'
It is a code. Every long phrase has a short translation.
regarding, concerning
+ genitive. 'Bezueglich Ihres Schreibens vom 10. Mai...'\nAbbreviated: 'bzgl.'

A: Regarding your application, we inform you...
B: What does the notice say?
A: Regarding the deadline, there is a change.
B: What change exactly?
From 'Bezug' (reference) + '-lich.' The standard opening of bureaucratic correspondence. You will find 'bezüglich' in the first line of nearly every official German letter.
with regard to, concerning
+ genitive. 'Hinsichtlich der neuen Verordnung...'\nSlightly more formal than bezüglich.

A: With regard to the new regulation, we inquire.
B: What exactly would you like to know?
A: With regard to the deadlines, there is uncertainty.
B: The deadlines remain unchanged.
From 'Hinsicht' (regard, respect) + '-lich.' Used in legal and academic texts. The elevated 'regarding' for when bezüglich is not formal enough.
due to, on the basis of
+ genitive. 'Aufgrund Ihrer Beschwerde...'\nAlso written 'auf Grund' (two words).

A: Due to the decision, the proceedings are discontinued.
B: What does that mean for my application?
A: Due to your complaint, we are reviewing the case again.
B: Thank you for the information.
'Auf' (on) + 'Grund' (ground/reason). Standing on the ground of your complaint. The causal genitive preposition of legal German.
as a result of, due to
+ genitive. 'Infolge des Beschlusses...'\nMore consequential than aufgrund.

A: As a result of the storm, roads were closed.
B: How long will the closure last?
A: As a result of the notice, you must pay.
B: Can I file an objection?
'In' + 'Folge' (consequence). The direct chain: this happened BECAUSE of that. More emphatic than aufgrund. The preposition that traces cause to effect.
subject to, pending
+ genitive. 'Vorbehaltlich der Genehmigung...'\nNothing is final until the condition is met.

A: The contract is valid subject to approval.
B: What happens without approval?
A: Subject to the review, the result is final.
B: So we still have to wait.
From 'Vorbehalt' (reservation) + '-lich.' The conditional preposition: IF this is approved. Adds a legal safety clause. Nothing is guaranteed with vorbehaltlich.
according to
+ dative (postposed): 'dem Gesetz zufolge.'\n+ genitive (preposed): 'zufolge des Gesetzes.'

A: According to the report, the numbers have risen.
B: According to which report?
A: According to the annual report.
B: Then the data is probably correct.
'Zu' (to) + 'Folge' (following). The attribution preposition: who says so? Postposed with dative is more common. Preposed with genitive is more formal.
according to
+ dative or genitive. 'Laut Paragraph 5...'\nShort and efficient.

A: According to the law, you must register.
B: Within what deadline?
A: According to paragraph 17, within 14 days.
B: Understood.
Originally from 'Laut' (sound). Now: 'according to what it says.' Short, efficient, bureaucratic. The most compact way to cite a source in German.
with reference to
Prepositional phrase. + accusative.\n'Unter Bezugnahme auf Ihr Schreiben vom...'

A: With reference to your letter of May 3...
B: What do they reply?
A: With reference to the notice, we inform...
B: That sounds very formal.
The most bureaucratic prepositional phrase in German. It connects your response to their letter. A cross-reference machine that tells the reader: I am responding to THIS document.
in the sense/meaning of the law
Fixed legal phrase. + genitive.\n'Im Sinne des Paragraph 5 des Aufenthaltsgesetzes...'

A: In the meaning of the law, he is considered a citizen.
B: Which law exactly?
A: In the meaning of paragraph 12 of the criminal code.
B: That is very specific.
Not your personal interpretation. The law's interpretation. This phrase removes all ambiguity. When you read 'im Sinne des Gesetzes,' the definition that follows is legally binding.
in accordance with paragraph
Citation formula. Paragraph number = legal authority.\n'Gemaess Paragraph 23 haben Sie das Recht auf...'

A: In accordance with paragraph 23, you have the right to object.
B: Within what deadline?
A: In accordance with the legal remedy instructions: four weeks.
B: Then I will file an objection.
The citation formula of German law. Paragraph number equals legal authority. No opinion, only law. Every German court ruling is built on chains of 'gemaess Paragraph' references.
to raise an objection
Fixed legal phrase. 'Hiermit erhebe ich Einspruch\ngegen den Bescheid vom...'

A: I would like to raise an objection.
B: Against which notice?
A: Against the tax notice of May 3.
B: Please submit the objection in writing.
'Erheben' means to raise or elevate. You lift your objection up for the court to see. The formal legal act of objecting. Always schriftlich (in writing).
hereby I file an objection
Complete opening sentence of a formal objection letter.\n'Hiermit' (hereby) + 'einlegen' (to file/lodge).

A: Hereby I file an objection against the notice of May 3.
B: Your objection has been received.
A: How long does the processing take?
B: Usually four to six weeks.
The phrase that starts the legal process of challenging a Bescheid. Memorize this sentence, and you can write the opening of any German legal objection letter.
the attachment, the enclosure
Feminine noun. From 'anlegen' (to attach/create).\n'Siehe Anlage 1' (See attachment 1).

A: You will find the documents in the attachment.
B: Which attachment?
A: Attachment 1: form. Attachment 2: certificate.
B: Thank you, I received both.
Also means: a facility, a system, or an investment. In letters: the documents attached. 'Anlage' is printed at the bottom of every official letter to list what is enclosed.
the official language
Feminine noun. Amt + Sprache (language).\nAlso: the language STYLE of the Amt.

A: The official language is German.
B: What does that mean?
A: All applications must be in German.
B: Even for foreigners?
Article 23 of the Verwaltungsverfahrensgesetz: 'Die Amtssprache ist Deutsch.' All government communication must be in German. No exceptions. Translations are your responsibility.
the contribution regulations, fee schedule
Feminine noun. Beitrag (contribution) + Ordnung.\n'Gemaess der Beitragsordnung...'

A: According to the fee schedule, the contribution is 120 euros.
B: Can the contribution be reduced?
A: Only according to paragraph 5 of the fee schedule.
B: Then I will file a request for a reduction.
Rules about who pays how much. Every club, insurance, and institution has a Beitragsordnung. German love of Ordnung applied to money.
the extension, the grace period
Feminine noun. Nach (after) + Frist (deadline).\n'Eine Nachfrist von 14 Tagen wird gewährt.'

A: You missed the deadline.
B: Is there a grace period?
A: Yes, a grace period of 14 days.
B: Thank you. This time I will meet it.
You missed the first Frist. Here is your second chance. Do not miss this one. A Nachfrist is a gift from the bureaucracy. They rarely grant a third.
the compensation, remuneration
Feminine noun. From 'vergueten' (to compensate).\nMore formal than Gehalt (salary).

A: The compensation is 3,500 euros gross.
B: Is that negotiable?
A: According to the compensation regulations, unfortunately not.
B: Then I accept the conditions.
From 'vergueten' (to compensate). Used in contracts and official documents. More formal than Gehalt. Public sector employees receive Verguetung according to fixed pay scales.
accordingly, therefore
Logical connector. 'Demnach ist die Frist einzuhalten.'\nShort, authoritative, legal register.

A: The deadline ends on June 15.
B: Accordingly, I must submit the application before then.
A: Correct. Accordingly, you have one week left.
B: I will submit it tomorrow.
'Dem' (that) + 'nach' (after/according to). Given the above, this follows. Short and conclusive. The lawyer's 'therefore.'
furthermore, in addition
Formal connector. 'Ferner ist zu beachten, dass...'\nMore formal than ausserdem.

A: The deadline must be met.
B: Furthermore, we request written confirmation.
A: Are there further requirements?
B: Furthermore, a copy of the ID must be enclosed.
'Fern' (far) + comparative. The formal 'also.' Legal and academic register. When 'ausserdem' is too casual, 'ferner' steps in.
accordingly, correspondingly
Also used as adjective. + dative.\n'Entsprechend der Vorschrift...'

A: In accordance with the regulation, the form must be filled out.
B: Which form?
A: In accordance with paragraph 12, form A3.
B: Understood. I will fill it out.
From 'entsprechen' (to correspond). The preposition of conformity: matching what is prescribed. As adjective: 'die entsprechenden Unterlagen' (the corresponding documents).

At B1 you learned während, wegen, trotz, statt + genitive. Now add:

bezüglich (bezgl.): bezüglich des Antrags
hinsichtlich: hinsichtlich der Frist
aufgrund: aufgrund der Beschwerde
infolge: infolge des Beschlusses
vorbehaltlich: vorbehaltlich der Genehmigung

All take the genitive. In spoken language some also accept dative. In written official German: always genitive.

Gemaess, laut, and zufolge fluctuate between dative and genitive. In formal writing, genitive is preferred.

Gemaess dem Gesetz (dative, correct)
Gemaess des Gesetzes (genitive, very formal)
Laut Paragraph 5 (without article, common)
Laut des Berichts (genitive, formal)
Dem Gesetz zufolge (dative, postposed)

Rule of thumb: at the office, always genitive. In conversation, dative is allowed.
Bezueglich + Genitiv = regarding. The standard opening of official correspondence.
Because of, on the basis of. A genitive preposition expressing a cause.
Due to the new regulation, changed deadlines apply.
According to the Bescheid (genitive preposition) + the application IS already approved (Zustandspassiv).
This form of 'sein' appears in formal official writing to create institutional distance, not to express a hypothesis or a command.
Hildi needs help with the digital Finanzamt. Verumius explains the online forms to her. Learn contract and complaint vocabulary, practice all three grammar constructions in mixed contexts.
Learn vocabulary for online forms and contracts
Practice all three grammar constructions in combination
Master complaint and contract vocabulary
Hildi's apartment. An immaculate living room. Doilies on every surface. Grandfather clock ticking. But on the table: a tablet. The tax office has gone digital. And Hildi needs help.
This technology. In the old days, you wrote a letter. Now I am supposed to type on a screen?
I will show you, Hildi. You taught me the recycling rules. Now I will show you the digital tax return.
All right. But only because the letter to the editor also has to be online.
the contract
Masculine noun. From 'vertragen' (to bear/agree).\nPlural: Verträge. 'Einen Vertrag unterschreiben.'

A: Have you read the contract?
B: Yes, all 20 pages.
A: Then please sign here.
B: The contract is hereby signed.
Both sides bear (tragen) the agreement together. 'Mietvertrag' (rental), 'Arbeitsvertrag' (employment), 'Kaufvertrag' (purchase). Every major life event in Germany involves a Vertrag.
the termination, the cancellation
Feminine noun. From 'kündigen' (to give notice).\n'Die Kuendigung des Vertrags' (contract termination).

A: I would like to submit my termination.
B: The notice period is three months.
A: Must the termination be in writing?
B: Yes, always in writing and signed.
German notice periods are among the strictest in Europe. Written, signed, fristgerecht. A verbal Kuendigung has no legal force. Always keep a copy.
the power of attorney
Feminine noun. Voll (full) + Macht (power).\n'Eine Vollmacht erteilen' (to grant power of attorney).

A: I hereby grant you power of attorney.
B: For which matters?
A: Power of attorney for all tax matters.
B: I will handle the case immediately.
Full power given to someone to act on your behalf. Legal authority transferred. Without a Vollmacht, no one can access your files at the Behörde. Not even your spouse.
the defect, the flaw
Masculine noun. Plural: Maengel.\n'Einen Mangel reklamieren' (to complain about a defect).

A: The device has a defect.
B: What defect exactly?
A: The screen has pixel errors.
B: Please file a complaint.
From Latin 'mancus' (deficient). In consumer law: if something has a Mangel, you have rights. Gewährleistung (warranty) covers Maengel for two years.
the damage, the harm
Masculine noun. Plural: Schaeden.\n'Einen Schaden melden' (to report damage).

A: I would like to report damage.
B: What kind of damage?
A: Water damage in the apartment.
B: We will send someone immediately.
Insurance vocabulary: Schaden is what happened, Schadensersatz is what you get. 'Sachschaden' (property damage) vs. 'Personenschaden' (injury). Germany's insurance culture revolves around Schaeden.
the complaint (about product/service)
Feminine noun. From Latin 'reclamare' (to cry out against).\n'Eine Reklamation einreichen.'

A: I would like to file a complaint.
B: What is the reason?
A: The product has a defect.
B: Please fill out this form.
The consumer's weapon. You have two years for Reklamation in Germany. Something is wrong? File a Reklamation. The law is on your side.
the formal warning
Feminine noun. Ab (off) + Mahnung (warning).\nIn employment: precedes Kuendigung.

A: Mr. Mueller received a formal warning.
B: For which violation?
A: Repeated lateness.
B: The next warning leads to termination.
The yellow card before the red. In employment law, an Abmahnung precedes Kuendigung. In consumer law: a cease-and-desist letter. Always take an Abmahnung seriously.
the service
Feminine noun. Dienst (service) + Leistung (performance).\n'Dienstleistungsvertrag' (service contract).

A: What service do you offer?
B: Tax consulting and bookkeeping.
A: What does the service cost?
B: According to our fee schedule.
Germany's economy is 70% Dienstleistungen. The compound says it: serving + performing. 'Dienstleistungsgesellschaft' (service economy) describes modern Germany.
the subject line
Masculine noun. From 'betreffen' (to concern).\n'Betreff: Ihr Antrag vom 10. Mai.'

A: What do I write in the subject line?
B: Subject: Objection against notice of May 3.
A: Must the subject line be so precise?
B: Yes, without a subject line, nobody can find your letter.
Every German official letter begins with Betreff. It tells the reader what the letter is about before they read a single sentence. Precision before content.
the letter to the editor
Masculine noun. Leser (reader) + Brief (letter).\nHildi's weapon of choice.

A: Hildi is writing a letter to the editor.
B: What is she complaining about?
A: About the new waste regulation.
B: Her letter will definitely be published.
Hildi's weapon of choice: disagreeing publicly through the newspaper. German Leserbriefe have a long tradition. Many local papers have a full page dedicated to them.
the task, the duty
Feminine noun. Auf (up) + Gabe (giving).\nAlso means 'giving up' (surrender).

A: It is the duty of the authority to inform you.
B: And my task?
A: Your task is to submit the application on time.
B: Understood. Everyone has their task.
Dual meaning: what you must do (task) or what you stop doing (surrender). 'Aufgabe' in bureaucracy always means duty. In chess, 'Aufgabe' means resignation. Context decides.
the requirement, the demand
Feminine noun. An + Forderung (demand).\n'Die Anforderungen erfuellen' (to meet the requirements).

A: Does the application meet all requirements?
B: Almost. A signature is still missing.
A: The requirements are strict.
B: Without all requirements, nothing will be processed.
What the system demands of you before it acts. Anforderungen are non-negotiable. Missing one Anforderung can delay your entire Antrag by weeks.
to apply for
Not separable. 'Einen Ausweis beantragen.'\nThe verb form of Antrag.

A: I would like to apply for a residence permit.
B: What documents do I need?
A: Everything listed in the information sheet.
B: I will apply for it immediately.
You beantragen, and the Behörde bewilligt (or ablehnt). The beginning of every bureaucratic journey. Without beantragen, nothing happens in Germany.
to process, to handle
Not separable. 'Ihr Antrag wird bearbeitet.'\nThe Sachbearbeiter bearbeitet your case.

A: How long does it take to process the application?
B: Usually four to six weeks.
A: Can it be faster?
B: Unfortunately not. We process all applications in order.
The process between submission and decision. 'Be-' + 'arbeiten' (to work). The Sachbearbeiter bearbeitet. When you hear 'Ihr Antrag wird bearbeitet,' prepare to wait.
to submit, to file
Separable. Ein (in) + reichen (to hand/reach).\n'Den Antrag einreichen.'

A: When must you submit the documents?
B: By June 15.
A: Have you submitted everything?
B: Yes, everything has been submitted on time.
You reach the document INTO the system. 'Fristgerecht eingereicht' means submitted on time. Missing the deadline means your submission does not count.
to approve, to authorize
Not separable. From 'genehm' (agreeable).\n'Den Antrag genehmigen.'

A: Was the application approved?
B: Yes, the approval has been granted.
A: Who approved it?
B: The responsible authority.
The Behörde finds your request genehm (agreeable) and says yes. The most welcome word in bureaucracy. 'Genehmigt' stamped on your Antrag is the German equivalent of a green light.
to issue, to exhibit
Separable. Aus (out) + stellen (to place).\n'Einen Bescheid ausstellen' (to issue a notice).

A: Who issues the notice?
B: The tax office issues it.
A: How long does the issuance take?
B: After approval, about two weeks.
The document is placed OUT of the system into your hands. Also used for exhibitions: 'Ein Bild ausstellen' (to exhibit a painting). Two meanings: to create an official document, or to display art.
to confirm
Not separable. From 'staetigen' (to make firm).\n'Den Empfang bestätigen' (to confirm receipt).

A: Can you confirm receipt?
B: Hereby we confirm the receipt of your documents.
A: Will I get a written confirmation?
B: Yes, the confirmation will be delivered by mail.
Making something firm, certain, official. Your signature bestätigt your identity. An Eingangsbestätigung (confirmation of receipt) is your proof that the Behörde has your documents.
to order, to decree
Separable. An (at) + ordnen (to arrange).\n'Die Behörde ordnet an, dass...'

A: The authority has ordered an inspection.
B: Who ordered that?
A: The responsible case worker.
B: You can file an objection against that.
An official command from an authority. Stronger than 'bitten' (to request). When the Behörde anordnet, it is not a suggestion. Compliance is expected.
to commission, to instruct
Not separable. Be- + Auftrag (commission/task).\n'Einen Rechtsanwalt beauftragen.'

A: I have commissioned a lawyer.
B: With what task?
A: He should formulate my objection.
B: That is a good decision.
You give someone an official Auftrag. Beauftragen is how professionals are hired in German legal culture. Without Beauftragung, no one acts on your behalf.
to file, to lodge
Separable. Ein (in) + legen (to lay).\n'Widerspruch einlegen' (to file an objection).

A: I would like to file an objection.
B: The deadline is four weeks.
A: Where do I file the objection?
B: In writing at the responsible authority.
You lay your objection INTO the legal process. 'Widerspruch einlegen,' 'Berufung einlegen,' 'Einspruch einlegen.' The verb for entering the legal arena.
to issue (a decree), to waive (a debt)
Not separable. Er- + lassen (to let).\nDual meaning: creating rules OR releasing from obligation.

A: The government has issued a new regulation.
B: What does it state?
A: Furthermore, some fees were waived.
B: So fewer costs for citizens.
Creating rules or releasing from them. 'Eine Verordnung erlassen' (to issue an ordinance). 'Die Schulden erlassen' (to waive the debts). The same verb, opposite effects.
Count each distinct grammatical construction: how the sentence opens, how the action is expressed without a named subject, and how the result state is described.
The separable verb that pairs with Widerspruch to form the standard legal objection formula. 1st person singular; prefix separates to the end.
Hereby I file an objection against the notice of May 3.
Regarding (genitive preposition) your application... the approval IS granted (Zustandspassiv).
Wird (werden) = process currently happening. Ist (sein) = result that is established. Two passives, two perspectives.
Test everything from Unit 30. Prove you can read, write, and respond to official German. Master the vocabulary of the Amt.
Test the complete vocabulary of Unit 30
Practice all three grammar constructions in combination
Prove competence in bureaucratic language
to prohibit, to forbid
Not separable. Unter + sagen (to say).\nMore formal than 'verbieten.'

A: The authority has prohibited the construction.
B: For what reason?
A: Due to a missing permit.
B: Can one take action against the prohibition?
The official word for banning something. More formal than 'verbieten.' When the Behörde untersagt, it is a legal act with consequences.
to arrange, to initiate
Not separable. Ver- + Anlass (occasion/cause).\n'Bitte veranlassen Sie die Überweisung.'

A: Please arrange the payment.
B: The payment will be arranged immediately.
A: Who initiated the proceedings?
B: The responsible authority.
You cause something to happen through official channels. Not doing it yourself, but setting it in motion. The bureaucratic 'make it happen.'
to decree, to dispose, to have at one's disposal
Not separable. Ver- + fuegen (to join/dispose).\nDual meaning: to command AND to possess.

A: The authority has decreed the closure.
B: From when does the decree take effect?
A: Immediately.
B: We have no other options at our disposal.
Dual meaning: to command AND to possess. 'Über etwas verfügen' (to have at one's disposal). 'Eine Schliessung verfügen' (to decree a closure). Context determines everything.
to refuse, to deny
Not separable. Ver- + weigern (to refuse).\nStronger than ablehnen.

A: The official refused the permit.
B: With what justification?
A: The documents were supposedly incomplete.
B: That is not comprehensible.
Stronger than ablehnen. Official refusal with legal consequences. When permission is verweigert, you need a Rechtsbehelf (legal remedy) to fight back.
to enforce, to execute (a judgment)
Not separable. Voll (full) + strecken (to stretch).\nThe final step of legal enforcement.

A: The judgment is now being enforced.
B: What does that mean in practice?
A: The claim will be forcibly collected.
B: Can the enforcement still be stopped?
The law stretches out fully and is applied. The final step: after Urteil comes Vollstreckung. 'Zwangsvollstreckung' (forced enforcement) is the last resort.
to provide for, to stipulate
Separable. Vor (before) + sehen (to see).\n'Das Gesetz sieht vor, dass...'

A: What does the law provide for?
B: The law provides for a deadline of four weeks.
A: Is that binding?
B: Yes, the law provides for no exceptions.
The law looked ahead and planned for this. 'Vorsehen' in legal language: the law stipulates, provides, foresees. What the law vorsieht, you must follow.
to reject, to dismiss
Separable. Zurück (back) + weisen (to direct).\n'Den Einspruch zurückweisen.'

A: The court dismissed the objection.
B: For what reason?
A: The objection was not filed on time.
B: Then only the appeal remains.
The authority sends your objection back to you. Rejected. Stronger than ablehnen. When something is zurückgewiesen, the process is over at this level.
to deliver (officially)
Separable. Zu (to) + stellen (to place).\nLegal delivery that starts Fristen.

A: The notice has been delivered to you.
B: When was it delivered?
A: On June 1. The deadline starts from delivery.
B: Then I have three more weeks.
Legal delivery: the moment of Zustellung starts all Fristen. A letter that is zugestellt has legal force. 'Zustellung per Einschreiben' (delivery by registered mail) is the standard.
to agree, to consent
Separable. Zu (to) + stimmen (to vote/tune).\n+ dative: 'dem Antrag zustimmen.'

A: Do you agree with the proposal?
B: Yes, I agree.
A: The authority agreed to the application.
B: Then the approval is granted.
You tune your voice TO the proposal. + dative. When the Behörde zustimmt, your Antrag is on the way to Genehmigung.
to revoke, to withdraw
Not separable. Wider (against) + rufen (to call).\n'Die Genehmigung widerrufen.'

A: The permit was revoked.
B: Why?
A: Due to new findings.
B: Can the revocation be challenged?
You call AGAINST your own previous statement. A Widerruf cancels what was granted before. 'Widerrufsrecht' (right of withdrawal) gives consumers 14 days to cancel online purchases.
to complain, to make a claim
Regular -ieren verb. Consumer rights vocabulary.\n'Ein defektes Produkt reklamieren.'

A: I would like to make a complaint about this product.
B: What is the defect?
A: It no longer works.
B: Do you still have the receipt?
From Latin 'reclamare.' Consumer rights vocabulary. You have two years for Reklamation in Germany. Always keep the Kassenbon (receipt).
to ratify
Regular -ieren verb. International law vocabulary.\n'Den Vertrag ratifizieren.'

A: The parliament ratified the treaty.
B: What does the ratification mean?
A: The treaty is now legally binding.
B: From when does it take effect?
From Latin 'ratum facere' (to make settled). A formal confirmation that makes an agreement binding. Only used for international treaties and major agreements.
to grant, to uphold
Separable. Statt (place) + geben (to give).\n'Dem Einspruch stattgeben' (to uphold the objection).

A: Did the court uphold the objection?
B: Yes, the objection was upheld.
A: What happens now?
B: The original notice is annulled.
The court gives the objection its place: granted. The opposite of zurückweisen. When a Gericht stattgibt, you win.
to comply with, to fulfill
Separable. Nach (after) + kommen (to come).\n'Der Aufforderung nachkommen.'

A: Please comply with the request.
B: Which request?
A: You should submit the documents.
B: I will comply with the request immediately.
You come AFTER the request, fulfilling it. + dative. When the Behörde fordert, you kommen nach. Compliance is not optional.
to remind, to send a dunning notice
Regular verb. The verb behind Mahnung.\n'An die Zahlung mahnen.'

A: We hereby remind you of the outstanding payment.
B: Which payment?
A: The invoice from April 15.
B: I will transfer immediately.
Three Mahnungen, then legal action. The escalation system of German payment culture. First friendly, then firm, then final.
to give notice, to terminate
Not separable. From 'Kunde' (notice/knowledge).\n'Den Vertrag kündigen.'

A: I would like to terminate my contract.
B: The notice period is three months.
A: Must I give notice in writing?
B: Yes, only written terminations are valid.
'Jemandem kündigen' (to fire someone). 'Selber kündigen' (to quit). German Kuendigungsfristen (notice periods) are among the longest in Europe. Always in writing.
to enclose, to settle (a dispute)
Separable. Bei (by/with) + legen (to lay).\nTwo meanings: attach OR resolve.

A: Please enclose the documents.
B: Which documents exactly?
A: Copy of the ID and registration certificate.
B: Everything enclosed. The dispute is also settled.
Two meanings: attach (to a letter) or resolve (a dispute). 'Die Unterlagen beilegen' (to enclose documents). 'Den Streit beilegen' (to settle the dispute). Same verb, different contexts.
in accordance with duty
Pflicht (duty) + gemaess (in accordance with).\n'Pflichtgemaess erfuellt.'

A: Have you fulfilled your duties in accordance with duty?
B: Yes, everything was completed on time.
A: The task has been completed as required.
B: Then the case is settled.
You did what duty required. The bureaucratic seal of compliance. 'Pflichtgemaess' is the adjective that confirms: all rules were followed.
submitted (past participle)
Past participle of einreichen.\n'Der eingereichte Antrag' (the submitted application).

A: The submitted application is being reviewed.
B: When was it submitted?
A: On June 1. The application was submitted on time.
B: Then only waiting remains.
Zustandspassiv ready: 'Der Antrag ist eingereicht' (the application has been submitted). The past participle that confirms: the document is in the system.
Count three distinct constructions: how the sentence is introduced, how the communication happens without naming a person, and how the approved state is expressed.
The court ruled in favor of the appeal. Choose the Partizip II that means the opposite of 'zurückgewiesen' (rejected).
The court upheld the objection.
Ist abgelaufen = IS expired (state). The deadline is past. The running has ended.
The formal objection formula uses a separable verb. The first blank is the 1st person singular conjugation; the second blank is the noun naming the formal legal objection.
A genitive preposition expressing a condition. The regulation does not take effect unconditionally; it depends on something else first.
Verumius helps Hildi with the digital forms. Role reversal. Complete your bureaucratic toolkit, experience a milestone, and review all constructions from Unit 30.
